citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
Male miniature Australian Shepherd (“Ruger”, microchip 220574, DOB 09/2018) has poor dental health. The upper and
lower cheek teeth on both sides of the mouth are completely encased in a thick brown tartar and the majority of the
surface of both upper canine teeth are also covered in tartar. A strip of grey material is located along the gum line of
several of the upper right cheek teeth and at least one lower left cheek tooth. One upper right cheek tooth is loose and
moved easily when gently manipulated by the inspector. Several areas of the upper right gums have receded away from
the teeth and both the upper and lower gums are slightly reddened.
The dog received a physical examination by the Attending Veterinarian on March 19, 2026, but the results of the exam
were not recorded and no instructions for treatment of this dog's teeth were provided at that time. The facility does not
have a documented plan to maintain clean and healthy teeth and no treatment has been provided or is planned for the
current condition of the teeth.
Each dealer must use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. This dog must
be evaluated by a veterinarian for an accurate diagnosis and to establish an effective treatment plan. The applicant must
ensure that each dog receives adequate veterinary care at all times.
Correction date: End of business May 30, 2026 or prior to your next scheduled relicense inspection whichever comes first.

The 2 trash cans in the housing facility do not have lids. Open trash receptacles could create odors and attract pests such
as flies and mice which could have a negative impact on the health of the dogs.
Trash containers in housing facilities must be leak-proof and have tightly fitted lids. The applicant must add a tightly fitted
lid to each trash can to ensure that potential odors are contained and to eliminate the possible attraction of pests.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
At least 2 enclosures, containing a total of three adult dogs, have a heavy buildup of brown dirt and organic material on
the access door and on the frame of the access door. A buildup of dirt and organic material could increase the risk of
disease hazards towards the dogs.
Used primary enclosures for dogs must be sanitized at least every 2 weeks and more often if necessary to prevent the
accumulation of dirt, debris, food waste, excreta, and other disease hazards. The applicant must remove the buildup of
dirt and organic material from the access doors and the door frames and ensure all surfaces remain clean and sanitized
to protect the health of the dogs.

The facility does not have preventative care and treatment plans documented in the Program of Veterinary Care to ensure
healthy and unmatted hair coats, properly trimmed toenails, and clean and healthy teeth, ears, eyes, and skin. Failure to
document preventative care and treatment plans may interfere with the ability of APHIS officials to determine if the dogs
are receiving veterinary care as directed by the Attending Veterinarian.
Each dealer must follow an appropriate program of veterinary care that is documented in writing and approved by the
Attending Veterinarian. The program of veterinary care must include preventative care and treatment plans to ensure
healthy and unmatted hair coats, properly trimmed toenails, and clean and healthy teeth, ears, eyes, and skin. The
applicant must consult with the Attending Veterinarian to develop these preventative care and treatment plans and add
the approved plans to the Program of Veterinary Care.

The adult dogs each received a physical examination by the Attending Veterinarian on March 19, 2026, but the results of
the exams were not recorded. Failure to records the results of the exams could interfere with the ability of APHIS officials
to determine if the dogs are receiving adequate veterinary care and to evaluate the effectiveness of the facilities
preventative and treatment plans.
Dealers must keep copies of medical records for dogs, and these records must include the dates and findings/results of
any screenings, tests and examinations. The applicant must ensure that the results of any physical examinations
conducted on the dogs is documented and available for review by APHIS officials.
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class "A" license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the
applicant.
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspection(s) or by July 26, 2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection.
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2)  Direct
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
Male miniature Australian Shepherd (“Ruger”, microchip 220574, DOB 09/2018) has poor dental health. The upper and 
lower cheek teeth on both sides of the mouth are completely encased in a thick brown tartar and the majority of the 
surface of both upper canine teeth are also covered in tartar. A strip of grey material is located along the gum line of

moved easily when gently manipulated by the inspector. Several areas of the upper right gums have receded away from 
the teeth and both the upper and lower gums are slightly reddened.  
 
The dog received a physical examination by the Attending Veterinarian on March 19, 2026, but the results of the exam 
were not recorded and no instructions for treatment of this dog's teeth were provided at that time. The facility does not 
have a documented plan to maintain clean and healthy teeth and no treatment has been provided or is planned for the 
current condition of the teeth.  
 
Each dealer must use appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. This dog must 
be evaluated by a veterinarian for an accurate diagnosis and to establish an effective treatment plan. The applicant must 
ensure that each dog receives adequate veterinary care at all times. 
 
Correction date: End of business May 30, 2026 or prior to your next scheduled relicense inspection whichever comes first. 
3.1(f) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
The 2 trash cans in the housing facility do not have lids. Open trash receptacles could create odors and attract pests such 
as flies and mice which could have a negative impact on the health of the dogs. 
 
Trash containers in housing facilities must be leak-proof and have tightly fitted lids. The applicant must add a tightly fitted 
lid to each trash can to ensure that potential odors are contained and to eliminate the possible attraction of pests. 
3.11(b)(2) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.

At least 2 enclosures, containing a total of three adult dogs, have a heavy buildup of brown dirt and organic material on 
the access door and on the frame of the access door. A buildup of dirt and organic material could increase the risk of 
disease hazards towards the dogs. 
 
Used primary enclosures for dogs must be sanitized at least every 2 weeks and more often if necessary to prevent the 
accumulation of dirt, debris, food waste, excreta, and other disease hazards. The applicant must remove the buildup of 
dirt and organic material from the access doors and the door frames and ensure all surfaces remain clean and sanitized 
to protect the health of the dogs. 
3.13(a)(4) 
   
Veterinary care for dogs. 
The facility does not have preventative care and treatment plans documented in the Program of Veterinary Care to ensure 
healthy and unmatted hair coats, properly trimmed toenails, and clean and healthy teeth, ears, eyes, and skin. Failure to 
document preventative care and treatment plans may interfere with the ability of APHIS officials to determine if the dogs 
are receiving veterinary care as directed by the Attending Veterinarian.  
 
Each dealer must follow an appropriate program of veterinary care that is documented in writing and approved by the 
Attending Veterinarian. The program of veterinary care must include preventative care and treatment plans to ensure 
healthy and unmatted hair coats, properly trimmed toenails, and clean and healthy teeth, ears, eyes, and skin. The 
applicant must consult with the Attending Veterinarian to develop these preventative care and treatment plans and add 
the approved plans to the Program of Veterinary Care. 
3.13(b)(4) 
   
Veterinary care for dogs. 
The adult dogs each received a physical examination by the Attending Veterinarian on March 19, 2026, but the results of 
the exams were not recorded. Failure to records the results of the exams could interfere with the ability of APHIS officials 
to determine if the dogs are receiving adequate veterinary care and to evaluate the effectiveness of the facilities 
preventative and treatment plans. 
 
Dealers must keep copies of medical records for dogs, and these records must include the dates and findings/results of 
any screenings, tests and examinations. The applicant must ensure that the results of any physical examinations 
conducted on the dogs is documented and available for review by APHIS officials. 
 
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class "A" license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the 
applicant. 
 
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspection(s) or by July 26, 2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee 
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection. 
 
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
